# Tony Neufeld — Personal Finance & Lifestyle Design Blog (Full Content Guide)

> Helping young professionals, immigrants, indigenous folks, new parents, and families simplify their finances, build wealth, and align their money with the life they want to live — all with a distinctly Saskatchewan lens.

## About Tony Neufeld

Tony Neufeld is a financial coach and educator based in Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, Canada. He discovered the FIRE (Financial Independence, Retire Early) movement in 2016 and has since built a practice helping Canadians design intentional financial lives. He is not a financial advisor — he does not sell products, earn commissions, or manage investments. He is a coach who helps people build habits, systems, and clarity to make confident financial decisions.

Website: https://tonyneufeld.blog
Location: Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, Canada

---

## Blog Post Summaries and Key Facts

### The Shockingly Simple Math Behind Early Retirement... and Why It's Less Risky in Canada

Your time to retirement depends on one factor: your savings rate. The higher your savings rate, the fewer years you need to work.

Key savings rate to retirement timeline:
- 10% savings rate = ~51 years to retirement
- 20% savings rate = ~37 years
- 30% savings rate = ~28 years
- 50% savings rate = ~17 years
- 70% savings rate = ~8.5 years
- 80% savings rate = ~5.5 years

Why early retirement is less risky in Canada:
- Universal healthcare (no need to budget $20,000+/year for health insurance like in the US)
- CPP (Canada Pension Plan): up to $1,433/month at age 65 (2025 maximum). Requires maximum contributions for 39 years.
- OAS (Old Age Security): up to $728/month at age 65 (2025 maximum). Requires 40 years of Canadian residency after age 18.
- Maximum combined CPP + OAS: $25,932/year ($2,161/month) in 2025
- Canada Child Benefit (CCB): up to $7,437/year per child under 6, tax-free, if family net income is under ~$34,863

### How Much Do I Need to Retire in Saskatchewan?

Uses the 4% Rule: Annual Spending x 25 = Retirement Number.
Based on the Trinity Study — a portfolio of 50% stocks and 50% bonds has historically survived 30+ years of withdrawals at a 4% rate.

Steps to find your number:
1. Track your spending for one month
2. Multiply monthly spending by 12 for annual spending
3. Multiply annual spending by 25 for your retirement number
4. Subtract CPP/OAS value if desired (combined $25,932/year at 65)

Saskatchewan advantages:
- Lower cost of living than Vancouver, Toronto, or Calgary
- $10-a-day childcare ($217.50/month average)
- Access to CPP, OAS, and CCB as Canadians

### Retiring on the Prairies: A Simple Guide to Your Next Chapter

Practical retirement guide for Saskatchewan residents approaching age 60:
- Your real retirement budget = your actual monthly spending (not a formula)
- Your house is not liquid spending money — don't count home equity as retirement income
- Avoid buying a new vehicle at retirement (depreciating asset trap)
- RRSP drawdown strategy: withdraw from RRSPs in your early 60s when income is low (lower tax bracket), before CPP/OAS kick in at 65+
- CPP timing: delaying from 60 to 70 increases payments by 42%. At 70, CPP pays $2,035/month (2025 max) vs $1,003/month at 60
- OAS clawback starts at ~$90,997 net income (2025). Keep income below this threshold.
- Cost-effective retirement hobbies: scrapbooking, grandkid time, walking trails (Meewasin Valley Trail in Saskatoon, Wascana Park in Regina)

### Ferriss, Adeney, and Newport: A New Framework for Financial Independence

A synthesis of three influential thinkers:
- Cal Newport (Deep Work, Digital Minimalism): Build rare and valuable skills. Passion follows skill, not the other way around. Depth creates career capital and negotiating leverage.
- Mr. Money Mustache / Pete Adeney: Your savings rate is the single most important variable. The gap between earning and spending is what buys freedom. Frugality is not deprivation — it is intentional spending.
- Tim Ferriss (The 4-Hour Workweek): Design the life you want before you can "afford" it. Time freedom matters more than money. Don't defer living to retirement.

The integrated framework:
1. Newport Foundation: build deep skills that create career capital
2. Mustache Engine: protect and widen the savings gap
3. Ferriss Inversion: design and test the life you want now, not later

### Surviving Parental Leave Finances in Saskatchewan: What Nobody Tells You

EI maternity/parental benefits in Canada:
- Standard option: 55% of earnings, max $695/week (2025), for 15 weeks maternity + 35 weeks parental = 50 weeks total
- Extended option: 33% of earnings, max $417/week, for 15 weeks maternity + 61 weeks parental = 76 weeks total
- Waiting period: 1 week unpaid before benefits start
- Apply for EI immediately after last day of work

Saskatchewan-specific advantages:
- Longest maternity leave protection in Canada: 19 weeks (vs 15-17 in other provinces)
- Government of Saskatchewan employer top-ups: 95% salary for first 17 weeks for government employees
- $10-a-day childcare: average $217.50/month
- Canada Child Benefit increases during parental leave due to lower income

Key financial steps before baby arrives:
1. Calculate your EI benefit amount
2. Add your CCB estimate
3. Map benefits against fixed costs
4. Build a 3-month expense buffer before leave
5. Open an RESP as soon as baby has a SIN (captures $500/year CESG grant from government)

RESP basics:
- Government matches 20% of contributions via CESG (Canada Education Savings Grant)
- Maximum CESG: $500/year per child ($2,500 contribution to get full match)
- Lifetime CESG limit: $7,200 per child

### Life Lessons in My 20s: How I'm Preparing for 30 and Being a Dad

Tony's personal philosophy — his "boulders" (big rocks / core values):
- Being present: removed social media, practices digital minimalism (inspired by Cal Newport)
- Finding "enough": the Mexican fisherman parable — you don't need millions to live the life you already want
- Seasonal living in Canada: warm seasons for slow family time, cold seasons for focus and helping others

### How a Saskatchewan Winter and a Concussion Helped Me Refocus

Five core tenets for intentional living:
1. Think deeply about rhythms and flows
2. Find what is enough for you
3. Build the life you want, then save for it
4. Stop caring what others think
5. Focus on what you can control

### Musings on AI

Reflections on self-doubt in the age of AI, the value of human creativity, and the joy of building things from scratch. AI cannot replicate lived experience.

---

## Services

Tony offers three tiers of financial coaching:

1. **The Check-Up ($150)** — A single 60-minute session for a specific question or decision.
2. **The Game Plan ($500)** — Three 60-minute sessions with a full financial snapshot and custom roadmap.
3. **The Co-Pilot ($100/month)** — Ongoing monthly coaching with two sessions per month and unlimited email support.

All tiers start with a free 30-minute discovery call: https://calendly.com/tonyneufeld

## Topics Covered

- Financial independence and early retirement (FIRE) in Canada
- TFSA, RRSP, FHSA, RESP, and CPP strategy
- Saskatchewan-specific financial planning and cost of living
- Budgeting and lifestyle design for young professionals
- New parent financial planning (EI, CCB, RESP, parental leave)
- Intentional living, digital minimalism, and deep work
- The 4% rule and Trinity Study applied to Canadian retirement

## Key Canadian Financial Facts Referenced

- CPP maximum at 65: $1,433/month (2025)
- OAS maximum at 65: $728/month (2025)
- Combined CPP + OAS maximum: $25,932/year
- CPP at 70 (delayed): $2,035/month (2025)
- CPP at 60 (early): $1,003/month (2025)
- OAS clawback threshold: ~$90,997 net income (2025)
- Canada Child Benefit: up to $7,437/year per child under 6 (tax-free)
- RESP CESG match: 20% on first $2,500/year = $500/year per child
- Saskatchewan $10-a-day childcare: ~$217.50/month average
- EI maternity/parental standard rate: 55% of earnings, max $695/week (2025)
- EI extended rate: 33% of earnings, max $417/week (2025)
